agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholder groups (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when and whether an order may be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, which progresses through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing it from being fulfilled and shipped."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The point at which an order's goods leave the warehouse for delivery. Represents a physical, irreversible state transition."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Per Finance, once created it is irreversible."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment a customer confirms an order. Per Operations, this is also the moment the order is invoiced."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations policy of generating an invoice at the exact time an order is placed, in order to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product policy governing the cancellation window. A VP could change this to a stricter or looser window.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, cancellation is permitted."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible — this is a financial integrity constraint. Cancelling an invoiced order would create accounting inconsistencies (revenue recognized, tax implications, audit trail corruption).","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, any cancellation attempt must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice later (e.g., at shipment) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice is created synchronously as part of the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says allow it (not yet shipped), BR-2 + BR-3 say deny it (already invoiced at placement). No correct behavior can be defined."},{"scenario":"Order placed but shipment delayed for days","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: The entire window between placement and shipment is simultaneously cancellable (BR-1) and non-cancellable (BR-2 + BR-3)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Cannot be resolved until the core contradiction is settled."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we follow BR-1 and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance faces revenue reconciliation failures, tax reporting errors, and audit risk. If we follow BR-2+BR-3 and block all cancellations, customers have zero ability to cancel, leading to increased chargebacks, refund requests, and customer attrition.","operational":"Whichever rule wins, the losing team's workflow breaks. Operations cannot invoice at placement if cancellation must remain open. Alternatively, Product cannot offer pre-shipment cancellation if invoicing is immediate and irreversible.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped yet. If cancellation is impossible from the moment of placement, customer satisfaction and trust will drop significantly. Support ticket volume will spike.","risk":"Implementing any interpretation without resolving this contradiction guarantees a broken business process. Either financial integrity is violated or customer-facing cancellation promises are false.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"If cancellation is blocked at placement, they lose all ability to cancel. Expect chargebacks, complaints, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation is allowed post-invoice, their books are corrupted. Irreversible invoices being effectively reversed creates audit and compliance exposure."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is moved later to resolve the conflict, their pricing-lock strategy breaks. They need an alternative mechanism to lock pricing."},{"group":"Product / PM","impact":"The promised cancellation feature cannot be built as specified without violating Finance constraints."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Regardless of outcome, support must handle the fallout — either explaining why cancellation is impossible or processing financial reversals."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment. BR-2 forbids cancellation after invoicing. BR-3 makes invoicing happen at placement — before shipment. Therefore the cancellation window (placement → shipment) is simultaneously open (BR-1) and closed (BR-2+BR-3). No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve this. Confidence is below 50 due to the direct contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — it is a logical impossibility","Each individual rule is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The domain terms (invoice, shipment, placement, cancellation) are well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's constraint should yield","Whether Finance would accept a 'credit note' or 'void' mechanism as distinct from 'cancellation'","Whether Operations could decouple pricing-lock from invoicing"],"assumptions":["Invoice and placement are truly synchronous — there is no gap between them","Invoicing irreversibility means no credit notes, voids, or reversal mechanisms exist","The PM's 'at any time before it ships' includes the moment immediately after placement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. BR-1 allows cancellation before shipment. BR-3 invoices at placement. BR-2 forbids cancellation of invoiced orders. Combined: every order is invoiced the instant it is placed, so no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1. The cancellation window defined by Product (placement → shipment) is completely nullified by the Finance + Operations rules.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Delay invoicing until shipment — preserves cancellation window (BR-1) but breaks Operations' pricing-lock strategy (BR-3)","B: Introduce a 'pro-forma invoice' or 'pricing reservation' at placement that is NOT a true invoice, then issue the real invoice at shipment — satisfies all three if Finance agrees a reservation is not an invoice","C: Allow cancellation with a compensating 'credit note' against the invoice rather than voiding it — preserves invoice irreversibility (BR-2) while enabling effective cancellation (BR-1)","D: Accept that cancellation is not possible post-placement and redefine the PM requirement to only allow cancellation during a brief pre-confirmation window"],"recommendation":"Option C is the most domain-appropriate resolution. In standard order management and accounting practice, credit notes exist precisely to handle this scenario — they preserve the invoice's integrity and audit trail while economically reversing the transaction. This satisfies Finance (invoice is never deleted or modified), Product (customer can effectively cancel), and Operations (pricing is locked at placement). However, all three stakeholders must explicitly agree."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated cannot be implemented — they form a logical contradiction","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellation before shipment) is impossible when BR-2 (no cancellation post-invoice) and BR-3 (invoice at placement) are both enforced"}]}
